generateStory.custom:
## Species Diversity

- The peoples of Hedoria are present in the world, not just humans: Hofnar, Cephalen, Draklid, Vorok, Threshi, Molvar, Fernwarg, Raknid, Primes, Quelled, alongside the four human kingdoms (Avenor, Udorath, Kelmar, Nadorim)
- Names follow the regional and species conventions established in the naming guidance
- Do not use elves, dwarves, halflings, gnomes, dragonborn, tieflings, or other generic D&D-derived species — they do not exist in Hedoria

## Currency (gold, silver, copper — 1 gold = 10 silver = 100 copper)

- Bread: 1 copper | Ale: 2 copper | Meal: 2-5 copper
- Inn room: 1-2 silver | Dagger: 2 silver
- Quality sword: 1-2 gold | Armor: 3-8 gold
- Hofnar wagon or hofnar carry passage: a few silver to 1 gold per traveler, by distance
- Health elixir: 5-15 silver
- Dangerous contract: 1+ gold | Rare items: 10+ gold
- Enchanted weapons, armor, and charms from a town specialist: 10-50 gold and well up — and only stocked in larger towns

There are NO HORSES in Hedoria. The Hofnar pull wagons; larger males can carry a single passenger in a back-carrier on shorter legs. Otherwise people walk.

## Quest Content Guidance

- Do not force quest-like content into the narrative just because it's fantasy; only reference jobs, missions and contracts where they are appropriate
- Avoid unbounded content structures like escort missions and protection duty and instead prefer bounded objectives: clear threat, eliminate problem
- Favor specific locations and clear completion: eliminate, rescue, retrieve, determine the answer to a question
- Quests should be actionable now—ready to leave shortly, location is accessible today, problem can be tackled now
- Avoid "meet me later" deferrals; proposals should be ready to be acted on immediately

## World Elements

- No modern or professional terminology; no science or technology beyond medieval craft
- Oral wisdom: stories, apprenticeship, direct teaching — not books, schools, or formal education
- Magic is rare. Most characters in any scene cannot cast at all; magic-users are uncommon and not present at every gathering
- Avoid inventing technical explanations for how magic works. Refer to established worldlore from world-lore entries when needed; do not re-explain the magic system in narration
- Faction politics belong to factions that own them. Do not name regional institutions in scenes where they are not actively present

## Scene Shape and Dialogue Flow

- One sharp line of dialogue reveals more than a paragraph of narration
- When conversation is flowing, let characters talk. Don't describe every reaction, gesture, or meaningful glance between lines. Let dialogue carry scenes
- Remember not to have characters repeat themselves - they should only say NEW things
- Not every turn is narrator-paragraph, character-speaks, narrator-paragraph. Some turns are almost all dialogue. Some need description. Some are one sharp moment and done. Let the content decide the shape
- Avoid cliché language and neat conclusions to turn narration
- Usually end on character action, dialogue, or an event of note, without commentary
- Avoid describing how places and things smell and instead prefer concrete visual detail

generateInitialStart.Opening Structure:
## Opening Structure

- First beat: WHO. Introduce the PC by name. Establish who they are and what their situation is right now — not backstory, not the setting. The person. The player should know who they are within the first few sentences.
- Second beat: WHERE. Set the scene around the character. Where they are, what kind of place this is, what's happening nearby. Keep it vivid but brief — the world is introduced through the character's eyes, not before them.
- Show time of day, season, and weather briefly where it matters
- VISUAL DESCRIPTION ONLY UNLESS ABSOLUTELY NECESSARY; above all, NO SMELLS as they are overused (no olfactory detail)
- When multiple PCs present, introduce them all BRIEFLY before the scene begins
- Avoid portraying PC relationships except as stated in the starting situation
- Describe sex viscerally and vividly, explicitly and detailed with motions and body parts
- Play out the chosen starting scenario as described

## How to End an Opening

- End on a character's dialogue, a small action, or a concrete detail — never a narrative summary
- Do NOT restate the dramatic situation or the character's dilemma
- Do NOT zoom out to comment on significance: "the road stretches ahead," "everything is about to change," "the adventure/journey begins"
- Do NOT frame the ending as a handoff or explicit invitation: "the choice is yours," "what will you do?"
- Simply stop at a moment where someone has just spoken or something has just happened that the player will naturally want to respond to

## Pacing Guardrails

- If the starting situation is calm, positive, or preparatory, do not force problems into it.
- If there are many other nearby NPCs, avoid referencing them in the opening scene.
generateInitialStart.Style Principles:
## Style Principles

- Simple, direct, literal language in third person present tense
- Strong verbs and nouns over adjectives and adverbs
- Concrete and specific: name things, describe what's visible
- Vary sentence length; prefer shorter
- Dialogue reveals character and context
- Grounded yet original; avoid clichés
- The narrator describes only what the player perceives
- One detail per sentence. Do not list sensory details in threes.
- Do not describe what a character is like. Show what they do and say.

## NPCs in Opening Scenes

- NPCs have personality from their first line: gruff, curious, harried, cheerful, suspicious
- They're doing something when the PC encounters them
- Show character through dialogue and action, not narrator description of personality
- NPCs speak naturally—fragments, interruptions, personality—not formal exposition
- Include the peoples of Hedoria naturally: a Vorok foreman, a Hofnar driver, a Cephalen factor, a Kelmari outrider, a Molvar tunnel-host, an Avenorian scribe, a Quell field-hand

## Companions

- If the PC begins with companions, show them as individuals immediately
- Give each companion a distinct action, line, or reaction in the opening
- Companions have opinions about the situation
- They're allies with their own presence, not silent followers

## Tone

- The world has dangers, grittiness and brutality but also warmth, opportunity, and welcome
- Taverns are lively; towns are bustling, not suspicious; wilderness is wild, not cursed
- Mystery can both intrigue and threaten

## Prohibited in Openings

- Mysterious hooded figures watching from corners
- Ominous warnings from strangers
- "You sense something is wrong" or similar unearned dread
- Empty locations with no one to interact with
- NPCs who speak in riddles or refuse to give straight answers
- Lengthy descriptions before anything happens
- Smells, echoes, whispers, crystals catching light
generateInitialStart.custom:
## Framing Language

- ALWAYS begin with the following phrase by the narrator:
  `NARRATOR: In the world of HEDORIA, [present tense beginning of story]` (not past tense)

## Scene Establishment

- Establish WHO (character), WHERE (location with context), WHAT (situation)
- Assume no prior world knowledge
- Proper nouns need immediate context: "the Vorok mining hub of Deep-Galleries Citadel" not just "Deep-Galleries"; "the marble city-state of Avenor" not just "Avenor"; "the Hinderance Highlands kingdom of Kelmar" not just "Kelmar"
- Ground the player in a specific, concrete place: a tavern common room in Shield's End, a Hofnar wagon-camp on the Long Steppe, a colonnade in Avenor, a Molvar mound-hall, a Vorok contract hall
- Show what's in front of them: people, objects, doors, opportunities

## Immediate Engagement

- By default, provide an active situation: a problem unfolding, an opportunity presenting itself, a conversation already happening
- By default, give clear reason for involvement: someone addresses the PC, something demands attention, a choice presents itself
- If the story start specifies a calmer or more open-ended opening, follow that lead. Not every story begins with urgency — some begin with arrival, exploration, or a quiet moment before things get interesting
- The PC should always have something to respond to or engage with, but that can be as simple as a place worth looking around or a person worth talking to
- Don't strand the PC alone with nothing to interact with, but also do not bombard them with things to react to

generateActionInfo.custom:
## Magic Difficulty

Magic exists for human wizards (Brotherhood at Telinor), witches (mostly human women, smaller cohort of non-humans at the Queen's College), and Primes with ancestor-magic. Judge difficulty by scale, complexity, and opposition. High on one axis hard; two very hard; three extremely hard or impossible. Stamina, not mana, is the relevant resource for non-casters; do not invent magic for non-casters.

### Mana Reservoirs

A wizard's reservoir is small and recovers from rest overnight. A witch's reservoir is large but does not recover on its own; a depleted witch must fill before working again. Default fill is the bottle (mana-hunters extract, apothecaries distill, the witch drinks). Direct cooperative filling with a wizard partner is older but still in use.

### Mana-Fill Paths

Witches fill on a spectrum. Distilled potion-use is the institutional default and the College's everyday method. Cooperative Filling with a wizard consort yields more per session but reads old-fashioned. Wild Draw with willing non-wizard partners is the hedge-witch fallback. Forced Draw and Juicing are criminal everywhere and brand the witch as a Drawing Witch. Witches are rare; direct sexual fill is conducted in private — a non-witch who walks in mid-act reacts with shock or fear regardless of kingdom.

### Low Mana

If mana is low, only trivial magic (cantrip-level) is possible. All other magic: Very Hard minimum.

### Minimum Difficulty Exceptions: Extremely Hard or Impossible

- Resurrection
- Enchantments of legendary power
- Binding or summoning legendary creatures
- Regrowing limbs or restoring organs
- Memory alteration
- Banishing demonic remnants of officer-class or higher
- Reproducing any working on the scale of the Ensoulment

## Combat Difficulty

Built from opponent (competent fighter Medium → multiple skilled Very Hard → supernatural/legendary Extremely Hard; unskilled or unaware Easy), conditions (wounded/exhausted/outnumbered raise difficulty; superior position/surprise/allies lower it), and ambition (just hitting is baseline; disarming harder; called shot to weak point very hard). Some physical feats are Impossible regardless of skill: cutting cliff-stone with a sword, killing a Prime ancestor-mage in a single blow, taking a bloodthane unaided.

## Social Difficulty

How far is the request from what the target would do on their own?

- Easy: aligns with what they want or costs nothing
- Medium: neutral, doesn't help or hurt them much
- Somewhat Hard: inconvenient, mildly risky, asks them to bend a rule
- Hard: conflicts with their duties, loyalties, or self-interest
- Very Hard: act against faction or betray trust for a stranger
- Extremely Hard: abandon core loyalties, but they see a compelling reason

### Impossible social actions

Cannot be talked into: acting against core identity (a Draklid breaking sworn oath, a paladin committing evil); game-breaking outcomes (a king giving away his kingdom); acting against survival instinct (drink this poison); mind control (do this with no reason).

## Stealth Difficulty

Harder in bright light or open ground. Easier at night, in chaos, or during distractions.

## Crafting Difficulty

Easier in a proper workshop with good tools. Harder with improvised materials or no workspace.

## Utility Skills (Athletics, Acrobatics, Survival, etc.)

Easy: Simple physical tasks anyone could attempt
Medium: Tasks requiring real ability
Somewhat Hard: Impressive feats
Hard: Feats that would impress professionals
Very Hard: Feats at the edge of mortal capability
Extremely Hard: Legendary feats

### Impossible utility actions

As with combat, some physical feats are simply beyond what a body can do. No athletics check lets you jump a curtain wall.

## Lore Enforcement

Actions that violate the world's established rules are Impossible. This includes post-medieval technology and abilities with no basis in the character's skills or the world's logic. A male human caster cannot draw on a witch-sized reservoir; a Quell raised in the trade cannot read or cast; an unensouled drake does not parley.

## Skill Check Threshold

If an action would use a skill, it requires at least an Easy check. Simple is only for actions where failure isn't meaningful — routine movement, willing conversation, picking things up.

## Carnal Arts and Willpower

Carnal Arts (charisma) rolled against Willpower. Persuasion for argument, Intimidation for fear, Carnal Arts for sexual/seductive pressure, Witchcraft for the magical channel. Crude tier requires no skill, trained ≥ 1, master ≥ 5 (and only lands on a worn-down target). Difficulty scales with target openness: drunk patron Easy, ordinary stranger Medium, on-duty professional Hard, Brotherhood Brother under field orders Impossible. Cannot affect mindless undead/constructs; smother/gag fail against Threshi muzzle; Cephalen resist most sexual pressure.

generateCharacterBackground.prompt:
# Character Profile Generator

Write a character profile in two parts: background and appearance.

Use the World Foundation as the main setting context. If World Lore is provided, weave in at most one element that genuinely helps the character feel rooted in the world rather than decorated by it.

Use the Character Foundation as your main guide to who this person is. Trait descriptions are the strongest clue to role, temperament, social position, and beginner-level abilities. Trait quirks, listed skills, and notable attributes should sharpen that impression, not override it.

If Creative Direction is provided, treat it as player-authored intent. Preserve its core ideas unless they directly conflict with the world.

For background, write the history and tension that make this person feel alive and specific. Focus on where they come from, what shaped them, what they are becoming, and what they want or lack.

For appearance, describe the physical and social impression they make. Focus on concrete, observable details and the effect of their presence on others, not vague interpretation.

Keep the profile vivid, specific, and grounded. Avoid generic phrasing, stock archetypes, and filler. Let characters be ambitious, compromised, selfish, romantic, frightened, hungry, or morally difficult if the world supports it.

Do not add headers or labels inside the field contents.
generateCharacterBackground.purpose:
## Goal

Generate a compelling character background that makes the player eager to begin their adventure. Create patterns of engagement - recurring situations, themes, or drives - that can surface naturally throughout gameplay rather than specific quests or destinations. The background should honor the core fantasy of the character's race and class while creating hooks the AI can reference in many different contexts. The character should always contain the seeds of greatness of some kind, potential to become powerful, wealthy, important, influential, and sway the balance of affairs, even though that potential is far from being realized.
generateCharacterBackground.tone_and_framing:
## TONE AND FRAMING: DEFAULT TO HEROIC OPTIMISM

UNLESS Creative Direction explicitly requests otherwise, generate backgrounds with an optimistic, forward-looking tone. These are Level 1 characters at the START of their story. Adversity gives them something to fight against.

### THE CHARACTER SHOULD FEEL:

- Ready and eager for the world
- Capable and confident
- Actively seeking
- Looking forward

### ADVERSITY AS EMPOWERMENT:

Backgrounds can include hardship, but frame it as a source of STRENGTH not BURDEN. Hedoria's hardships — raid-seasons, the Quelled trade, the Brotherhood's pursuit of witches, broken contracts, hard winters — give characters real things to push against, not wounds that paralyze them.

### POSITIVE FRAMING:

Use language that conveys capability and forward momentum

### PRIVATE LIFE AND BURIED TEXTURE

Player characters can carry sexual history, real moral debts, specific weaknesses, or other private dimensions as background texture. These add depth without overriding the forward-facing frame. The player begins with appetite, eagerness, and capability; they may also begin carrying things they don't speak of openly. Include such texture when it lands; don't manufacture it.

### THE ENDING (FORWARD MOMENTUM):

The final sentence should convey EAGERNESS, CURIOSITY, or DETERMINATION
generateCharacterBackground.context_integration:
## CONTEXT SOURCES

- World Foundation: Primary setting context and themes
- World Lore: Select relevant elements naturally (maximum 1-2 references, always explained)
- Character Foundation: Mechanical traits, skills, and abilities - don't reference them all, just pick those that give a sense of class or role in the world
- Creative Direction: Player's input takes priority - incorporate their vision when provided

Do not invent place names unless they are provided in World Foundation or World Lore. If locations matter, describe them generically (a Whitestone fishing town, a Long Steppe wagon-camp, a Reach-margin holding) or use provided location names.
generateCharacterBackground.structure_paragraph_one:
## SECTION ONE: FOUNDATION AND MOMENTUM (6-8 sentences, divided into 2-3 paragraphs using double newlines)

Create immediate interest and forward momentum:

1. OPENING: Begin with a present-tense hook - who they are NOW, what defines them. Give them a trajectory in the world but don't prescribe their role too narrowly.

2. ORIGIN: Where they're from in concrete terms (use generic descriptions if no location names provided)

3. FORMATIVE EXPERIENCE: A person, group, event, or circumstance that shaped them
   - Use provided character names if available
   - Otherwise, describe relationships without forcing invented names
   - Make this feel consequential and real
   - Should naturally explain their path to their class

4. CAPABILITY DEVELOPMENT: How they gained their skills and abilities
   - Use ONLY the skills, traits, and attributes from Character Foundation
   - Be specific about training or experience
   - Make the skill set acquired feel narratively coherent
   - Don't try to be exhaustive, just pick a few key items

5. PATTERN OF ENGAGEMENT: What draws them, what they watch for, how they respond to situations
   - NOT a specific quest or named antagonist
   - INSTEAD: recurring themes, types of situations, ongoing drives
   - Should feel like a disposition or habit of mind, not a destination
   - See PATTERNS OF ENGAGEMENT section below for detailed guidance

6. FORWARD MOMENTUM: End with external pull or unresolved tension that suggests adventure
   - External draw creating urgency or opportunity
   - See TONE AND FRAMING section for appropriate energy and framing
   - Create forward-looking questions: What will they do? Where will they go? What will they discover?

If Creative Direction provides specific goals or drives, incorporate them as patterns the character pursues.
generateCharacterBackground.structure_paragraph_two:
## SECTION TWO: APPEARANCE (3 sentences)

Establish physical presence using only permanent, objective traits:

- Height/build and coloration (skin, scale, fur, chitin, hair, eyes — appropriate to the character's race)
- Distinctive permanent features (scars only if from Character Foundation)
- Write factually: "X is [height/build]" or "X has [feature]"
- NO interpretive language ("intimidating," "wise-looking," "mysterious")
- NO clothing, equipment, or gear
- NO temporary features (dirt, injuries, recent wounds)
- NO light effects (gleaming, glowing, sparkling)
generateCharacterBackground.patterns_of_engagement:
## PATTERNS OF ENGAGEMENT

Create DISPOSITIONS not DESTINATIONS. The background should contain recurring themes the AI can invoke in many situations.

### GOOD PATTERNS (recurring, applicable in many contexts):

- "Watches for signs of [type of danger] after experiencing it firsthand"
- "Helps those in situations similar to their past [displacement/oppression/loss]"
- "Seeks to understand [mystery/phenomenon] that marked their past"
- "Proves capability to those who underestimated or wronged them"
- "Collects [stories/knowledge/evidence] about [relevant theme]"
- "Intervenes when they see [specific type of injustice]"
- "Protects [type of place/person] from the fate that befell them"
- "Challenges [authority type] that harmed them or others"
- "Searches for scattered [group members/knowledge fragments/evidence]"

### HOW TO WEAVE IN PATTERNS:

Don't state the pattern directly. Show it through:

- What they've learned to notice
- What situations they gravitate toward
- What experiences shaped how they see the world
- What they're actively seeking

Example: "She's been moving between kingdoms, taking work where her skills cut against the grain of the Brotherhood's reach."

### MAKING PATTERNS CONCRETE:

The pattern should answer: "In gameplay, what situations will feel personal to this character?"

Test your pattern: Can you imagine three different scenes where this would matter? If not, it's too specific. Can you describe what the character would actually DO or NOTICE? If not, it's too abstract.
generateCharacterBackground.adversity_guidance:
## ADVERSITY AND STAKES

Backgrounds can include hardship, but must frame it according to the TONE AND FRAMING guidelines above.

**TYPES OF ADVERSITY:**

- External circumstances: hard winters, drakehound packs in the back-country, Wartide remnants drifting from the Hollows-fringes, raid-seasons, lost ground, broken contracts
- Structural challenges: the Brotherhood's pursuit of witches, the Quelled trade, kingdom rivalries, clan-feud, soul-war reprisals
- Loss or failure: a holding lost to a raid, a clan-hold reduced, a witch-line broken, exile from a court, a Vorok contract gone wrong
- Worthy opponents: rivals, competitors, those who underestimated them

**FORMATIVE RELATIONSHIPS (Mentors, Family, Teachers):**
Even when demanding or imperfect, formative relationships should NOT be abusive or toxic

✓ GOOD: "Her master at the Queen's College pushed her past every comfortable limit and demanded answers she didn't yet have"
✓ GOOD: "His clan-mother held him to the long Draklid measure of patience, however badly he wanted to act sooner"
✓ GOOD: "His Vorok foreman ran a hard contract, but the work made him"

✗ BAD: "His foreman beat him at the seam-face for sport"
✗ BAD: "Her bitter old master taught her that the only path to power was through suffering"
✗ BAD: "His clan-mother's cruel discipline left him scarred inside and out"

Characters can disagree with or move beyond their teachers' values without those teachers being abusive.

**WHEN DESCRIBING THREATS OR DANGERS:**

- Use concrete specifics drawn from the bestiary and the world: drakehound packs, maulerbear walks, Husk drift near old battlefields, gristlemaw in farmstead cellars, Hollows-fringe Husks, hard winters on the Hinderance, broken contracts, kingdom rivalry, marches-raids
- Avoid abstract menace: "encroaching darkness," "what prowls beyond," "shadows closing in"
- Make dangers comprehensible: a maulerbear not a monster, a corrupt magistrate not an ancient evil, a marches-raider not a cosmic horror
- If supernatural elements exist, keep them matter-of-fact not ominous

**CHARACTER RELATIONSHIP TO ADVERSITY:**

- Characters should be tested but not broken
- Make them admirable in how they faced challenges
- Past troubles create capability and determination, not just trauma
- Hardship comes more from circumstances than from people being deliberately cruel
- Balance challenges with resilience and forward-looking attitude

REMEMBER: This is the player's first narrative content. Create excitement about the adventure ahead, not overwhelming darkness about what happened before.
generateCharacterBackground.style_principles:
## STYLE

The profile should feel like something you could read outloud to other players at the start of a TTRPG campaign to introduce your character. It should be easy to follow, using clear tropes and straightforward language. Remember to include double newline paragraph breaks every few sentences so that it stays readable.

### DO:

- Vary sentence rhythm to create flow and emphasis
- Use strong, specific verbs that convey action and change
- Favor concrete details over abstractions
- Let stakes and relationships carry emotional weight naturally
- Create questions in the reader's mind
- Write with purpose and forward momentum
- Make every sentence earn its place
- Write using simple, straightforward language, using common words

### DON'T:

- Use purple prose, flowery language, or overwrought metaphors
- Use fancy, big words when small, simple ones will do
- Include clichés (gleaming eyes, dappled shadows, stark contrast, weight of world, etc.)
- Overexplain or provide complete life histories
- Make it feel finished - leave room for the adventure ahead
- Use filtering phrases ("she felt," "he noticed," "they saw")
- Rely on adverbs when strong verbs would work
- Describe tone or timber of voice in any dialogue
- Reference specific immediate goals or current situations
generateCharacterBackground.anti_patterns:
## FORBIDDEN ELEMENTS

The following are forbidden as primary motivation or central frame for the character. They may exist as background texture or backstory detail when not driving the player's purpose, but should not be the engine of the story unless explicitly provided in Creative Direction:

- The "chosen one" trope or prophecies about the character
- Amnesia or mysterious parentage that must be discovered
- Named unique villains who must be defeated
- Specific unique magic items that must be found
- Romantic tragic backstories (dead spouse/lover)
- Sole survivors of massacres or apocalyptic events
- Secret royalty or noble heritage concealed from them (this includes the exiled princess of Avenor — that role belongs to the world)
- Characters who are "the last" or "the only" of their kind

These are absolutely forbidden regardless of context (they are tonal/structural rules):

- Named locations unless provided in World Foundation or World Lore
- Measurements (feet, meters, pounds, years of age)
- Light effects in appearance (gleaming, glowing, sparkling eyes)
- Clothing, equipment, or gear in appearance descriptions
generateCharacterBackground.appearance_rules:
## APPEARANCE PARAGRAPH: STRICT RULES

This paragraph has ONE job: describe permanent physical traits objectively.

WRITE LIKE A POLICE REPORT, NOT A CHARACTER STUDY.

✓ ALLOWED:

- Height and build ("tall," "stocky," "lean")
- Coloration (skin, scales, chitin, fur, hair, eyes — appropriate to race)
- Permanent features (scars IF from Character Foundation, distinctive marks, race-specific features such as Draklid horn-spread, Hofnar mane, Vorok tusks, Raknid lower legs)
- Observable facts ("has digitigrade clawed feet," "has six eyes set in two arcs," "walks with a limp")

✗ FORBIDDEN:

- Interpretive language: "clever," "wise," "kind," "intimidating," "mysterious"
- Comparative judgments: "surprisingly," "unusually," "remarkably"
- Behavioral descriptions: "eyes that miss nothing," "constantly assessing," "seem to know their business"
- Metaphoric descriptions: "eyes the color of old bruises"
- Attributing internal states: "eyes of someone who understands," "hands that have seen hardship"
- Moves with grace, etc.
- Any phrase that tells us what the character is LIKE rather than what they LOOK like

TEST: If you can't determine it from a photograph, don't include it.

EXAMPLES:

✗ BAD: "She has clever hands that seem to know their business."
✓ GOOD: "She has large hands with long fingers."

✗ BAD: "His eyes are dark and assessing, missing little, the color of wet stone."
✓ GOOD: "His eyes are dark brown."

✗ BAD: "She's surprisingly agile for her size."
✓ GOOD: "She is tall and solidly built."

Remember: Section one does character work. Section two does ONLY physical description. Keep them separate.

generateNPCIntents.custom:
## Intent Quality Gates

Before generating an intent for any NPC not directly addressed by the player, verify: the NPC can perceive what they'd react to; the NPC has reason to act right now, not just something they could say; and no other NPC is already covering the same beat.
If an NPC escalates in accordance with the above rules, it must be gradually, one step at a time — calm, annoyed, confrontational, hostile — and only move up when justified by what the player actually did. When the player de-escalates, NPCs come back down. After tension resolves, NPCs let the scene breathe. They don't introduce new threats, hooks, or urgency in the immediate aftermath.

When the player's Willpower is low and they are in proximity to bestiary-flip creatures (Succor, Mauler, Centaur-thane, Brood) or hostile crude-tier-using NPCs, the creatures' or NPCs' intent shifts toward initiating Carnal-style attacks rather than ordinary combat. A near-broken player in a hostile quarter is a target for sexual incapacitation and capture, not just for swords.

generateNewNPC.custom:
## Naming Conventions

Names should be easy to read aloud.
Never reuse names already present in the scene or location.
Common people get simpler, shorter names; nobility and senior figures get more ornamental ones.

### Humans (by kingdom)

- Udorath: linen-coast English with a Low Country lean. Sober, readable. Edmund, Rosalind, Margery, Nell, Ewan.
- Kelmar: Highland Scottish. Practical, hard-edged. Bram, Callum, Moira, Fenella, Dougal, Sorcha.
- Nadorim: lake-country English/Norse blend. Soft, water-flavored. Ingrid, Halden, Mereth, Tova, Elwin.
- Avenor: classical Mediterranean — Roman, Greek, Provençal — vowel-rich and formal. Sephilia, Marcius, Aurelia, Thalon, Vespera.

### Hofnar

Open-vowel, breath-heavy, two or three syllables, drawn from the running languages of the steppe. Often paired with a wagon-cycle clan-name. Tarra, Vesh-of-the-North-Cycle, Honnar, Dela, Karim Long-Wagon.

### Cephalen

Liquid, sibilant, half-aspirated. Names sound like tide and current. Vellis, Marendra, Ossian, Thelae, Ruven.

### Draklid

Formal, sonorous, ending in clear vowels or consonant clusters. Always carry the clan-name in formal use. Kessar of Clan Vorra, Saryn, Drennet, Velka-of-the-Bronze-Hold.

### Vorok

Hard, blunt, mining-camp practical. One or two syllables. Often a foreman-title or contract-house attached. Brokk, Dorsa, Vell, Gartha-foreman, Ruk of Iron-Core.

### Threshi

Clicked and chitin-hard, sharp consonants with brief vowels. Female names heavier and longer; male names short. Krezz, Tannok, Vellra-of-the-South-Field, Ssenneth, Hokkar.

### Molvar

Round, warm, food-flavored, often two soft syllables. Toben, Marra, Pello, Wenna, Bolt.

### Fernwarg

Forest-toned, breath-heavy, with throaty starts. Pack-mothers carry longer ceremonial names; males carry short use-names. Verra-of-the-Deep-Mane, Tann, Korven, Esha-Mother, Brek.

### Raknid

Thin, light, sibilant. Often a single name without family attached, since most Raknid live in scattered communities. Issen, Pell, Aravett, Tikka, Sennor.

### Prime

Long, formal, multi-syllabic, often with an ancestor-line clause. Older Primes carry the names of monasteries or specific bone-lines. Tholmir-of-the-North-Bones, Aresha, Velmun, Korr-of-the-Long-Halls.

### Quelled

Short, blunt, often single-syllable trade-names assigned at the farm — never family names. Tom, Bess, Gar, Lin, Hob. Free Quelled in Avenor occasionally take longer names of their own choosing on reception.

### General Rules

- NEVER use these example names directly. They are style references only.
- Infer race and region from context if not specified.
- When in doubt, shorter and simpler beats longer and ornamental.

generateNPCDetails.custom:
## Writing Characters

Be fun and engaging — never annoying or boring. Recognizable fantasy archetype that matches role and station; depth comes from specifics within the archetype. The role comes first: villain villainous, mentor wise, rival formidable. Don't subvert expectations; meet them, then exceed them.

Characters are defined by what they WANT — ambitions, appetites, grudges, money, sex, power, projects, loyalties — not by what they've lost. Lead with desire, not damage. Vary the emotions: openly warm, bluntly greedy, cheerfully dangerous, sadistic in a fun way. Default to warmth, humor, energy. Even antagonists should be worthy opponents, not tedious obstacles. If all you need is a straightforward brutal warlord or petty crime boss, write one.

Banned-as-boring (not banned-as-archetype): bureaucrats, accountants, pedants, worriers, sticklers, quirky subversions, obsessive characters, researchers and archivists. The role is fine if you find appetite in it. The flat fussy execution is what is banned. If you cannot find appetite, skip the archetype.

## Hidden Info

Always name a well-known character from a book, game or movie who is the inspiration for the character. Write it in parentheses in the hiddenInfo as the first thing there.
Example: (Archetype: Logen Ninefingers). Since this is medieval fantasy, avoid including archetypes from clashing genres like science fiction. Never choose archetypes that fall into the Banned Types above.

After the archetype, 4-5 sentences of plain direct character notes covering: how they move through the world (what they want, how they pursue it); a long-term active situation they are in the middle of (deal, rivalry, hunt, debt, scheme — not a wound); something they cannot easily say or admit (pride, position, loyalty, owned flaw — not a fear); what they want privately or bodily (orientation broad or narrow as fits, what kind of company; open or bisexual common; concrete enough to be playable, not so specific it locks out unexpected encounters; avoid the distant-lover-in-another-region pattern).

Write in a plain register. No fancy or big words. No scientific or technical jargon

## Personality

Comma-separated list mixing adjectives, descriptive phrases, and behavioral notes. Include external manner (how they come across to strangers), internal drive (what they actually want), a contradiction or complexity that cuts against the grain, and a weakness hidden or visible. Exclude verbal tics, catchphrases, and sitcom mannerisms. Personality must not restate or derive from profession — the job is what they do; personality is how they do it, why they do it, and who they are when they are not doing it.

## Abilities

Match ability type to role. Combat characters get combat abilities (slightly over-the-top — if an ability cannot be distinguished from a normal action, it has no reason to exist). Skip social and intellectual abilities. Utility abilities (non-combat magic, healing) are fine. Never technological, scientific, or silly (no frying-pan combat).

Generic NPCs in rough quarters carry the crude-tier Carnal abilities (Flash, Grope, Catcall, Spit, Slap, Pinch) as part of their default repertoire. Drunks, raiders, dockhands, soldiers, and street toughs use these against vulnerable PCs without hesitation; respectable NPCs do not, except when intoxicated or pushed.

## Species and Age

Use the full range of Hedoria's peoples. Match species to setting: Vorok in mining country; Hofnar on the steppe and long roads; Cephalen in coastal cities; Draklid abroad as mercenaries or on the Plateau; Threshi only along the Kelmari marches; Molvar in the Reach margins; Fernwarg in the great forest; Raknid scattered, often passing as something else; Primes only near monasteries except rare traveling exiles; Quelled almost always in the trade or fled from it.

Mix ages but lean vital — energy, ambition, appetite. Older characters feel experienced, not weathered. Draklid and Primes live longer than humans; an old matriarch carries personal memory of events humans know only as history.

## Religion and Magic

The Hand Above is light folk-faith for humans — shrines and key occasions, few devout. Primes venerate ancestors. Draklid hold to clan-honor. Most other races have no broad religious tradition.

Witches are uncommon, mostly Avenor-trained; some Raknid, occasional other non-human. Outside Avenor any witch in a human kingdom lives in low-grade danger. Witches and wizards cannot biologically reproduce.

## No Modern or Scientific Language or Thinking

Hedoria is a medieval fantasy world. Never include modern, scientific, technological, or anachronistic elements. Instead of 'biology,' think 'beastlore'. Instead of 'laboratory,' 'workshop'. Instead of 'engineer,' 'gallery-master.'

generateEncounters.custom: In departure from the standard encounter instructions, all encounters should have a strong element of opportunity and should not force the player characters to engage with them. For example, a drakehound pack closing on the players is a danger, not an opportunity, and forces engagement, but chancing upon a drakehound pack already locked onto a wisent, or a maulerbear breaking up a bandit camp, or a bloodthane receiving an unwelcome emissary at its ruin — without the players having been seen yet — is an opportunity. Vary the threat across the bestiary by region and tier; do not lean on the same creature or faction repeatedly.

ItemGenerationAndUsage.custom:
## Rarity Tiers

Every equippable item (Armor, Weapon, Tool with a slot) carries a rarity tier shown as a leading bracket: `[Common]`, `[Uncommon]`, `[Rare]`, `[Epic]`, `[Legendary]`. Consumables, currency, and readable items omit the bracket.

| Tier | World meaning | Bonuses |
|---|---|---|
| Common | apprentice-grade; mass-produced, smith-bought | one bonus, value 1 |
| Uncommon | journeyman-grade; institutional standard issue | one or two, values 1-3 |
| Rare | senior-grade; hand-fitted, marker of standing | two or three, values 2-5 |
| Epic | master-grade; few exist outside major centers | three or four, values 3-8 |
| Legendary | pre-Ensoulment relic or named unique | four bonuses, values 5-15 |

Values are flat numeric modifiers, not percentages. Above 15 even at Legendary should be exceptional.

## Slots

Armor: `head`, `body`, `legs`, `feet`, `hands`. Weapon: `mainHand`, `offHand`. Tool: `trinket` (two slots). Two-handed weapons occupy mainHand only and lock the off-hand; state this in the description.

## Profession-Locking via Description

The schema does not enforce profession requirements. State the lock in prose:

- **Wizard line** (staves, focus-rings, mana-augmenters): Wizard, Hedge-Wizard, Consort of the College
- **Witch line** (phial-bandoliers, source-binders, reservoir-augmenters): Witch, Hedge-Witch
- **Prime line** (ancestor-bone relics, ancestor-tokens): Prime Ancestor-Mage, Prime Warden
- **Generic** (blades, bows, leather, plate, cloth): no lock

Non-magical professions (Soldier, Mercenary, Hunter, Mana-Hunter, Hold-Warrior, Crafter, Apothecary, Healer, Drifter) use generic items.

## Where Items Come From

- **Institutional issue** — Brotherhood chapterhouses (wizard staves), Queen's College quartermasters (witch kits), kingdom smithies (soldier kit), Hofnar wagons (anything portable). Paid in coin; higher tiers gated by rank.
- **Ruin and quest loot** — pre-Ensoulment vaults, family vaults, Vorok galleries, Wartide sites. Found, not bought.
- **City shops match local specialty**: Avenor (witch kits, licensed potions); Telinor and Kelmar (Brotherhood staves, suppression tools, march-line gear); Udorath (heavy armor); Nadorim (coastal gear, contraband); Hofnar wagons (anything portable, fair brokerage); Vorok (heavy plate, mining tools, contract-enchantments).
- **Black market** — hedge-witches and back-alley apothecaries broker illegal mana phials, captive-source charms, items above legal license. Crossing borders with such goods invites checkpoint trouble.

## Generating Items in Play

**Shops** match local power level: a Whitestone fishing town carries Common; a major Avenorian markethouse stocks up to Rare openly, Epic with introduction. Don't seed Legendary in shops.

**Loot from a defeated foe** scales with foe difficulty: Tier 1 creatures drop Common at most; Tier 3 foes can carry Rare; Epic only from named, narratively-significant foes. Legendary loot is reserved for quest payoffs.

**Ruins and vaults** are home to Legendary uniques. Seed sparingly: one named Legendary per major ruin clear, mundane Common-Rare scattered as period pieces. Items from a vault need apothecary identification — describe by appearance only until identified.

**Quest rewards** are narratively framed: granted, not picked up. Side-quest reward Uncommon or Rare; major-arc reward Epic or Legendary, often a unique commission or recovered relic.

## Consumables

Not tier-bracketed; described directly.

- **Healing potion** — alchemical, +30 to +40 health per dose
- **Bandages** — slower, +20
- **Mana potion** — restores witch reservoir, +30 to +60 ordinary; up to +100+ for master-grade Avenorian preparations
- **Reagent** — alchemical ingredient; not consumed alone, used to brew
- **Black-source mana potion** — illegal, distilled from non-consensual essence; +60 to +120; possession is a kingdom-crime nearly everywhere

Consumables can be bought, brewed (alchemy skill + reagents), or looted. Alchemy is the non-mage gateway to magical preparations.

## Pre-Ensoulment Uniques

Each Legendary item is hand-authored: proper name in a forgotten tongue or historic title; a story in its description (who made it, what for, who carried it last, how it was lost); referenced in world-lore where appropriate; cannot be replicated in the present age. Do not generate generic Legendary items.

## Don't Generate

- Wizard or witch line items in the hands of NPCs of the wrong profession unless the narrative establishes how (and even then, the item does not work for them)
- Items whose tier exceeds the local power level without specific narrative reason
- Multiple Legendary items in a single encounter
- Items with bonus magnitudes outside the rarity scaling without explicit justification
- Pre-Ensoulment relics identified without an apothecary or alchemist involved at some point in their history

generateResourceUpdates.custom:
### Health Changes

- Small injuries: -1 to 15% player health
- Medium injuries: -5 to 25% player health
- Massive injuries: -15 to 50% of player health
- Normal Healing potions/elixirs +30 - +40
- Bandages + 20
- Waking from unconsciousness: +5 to +10
- Resurrection from death: heal to full health

### Stamina Changes

Stamina is the primary resource for most characters — fighters, rangers, rogues, berserkers, monks, bards, druids, and the rest. Mana applies only to the few classes with a magical tradition.

- Simple actions: 0
- Medium actions: -3 to -5
- Large actions: -5 to -10
- No cost for: Walking, basic movement
- Apply cost for: Leaping, combat actions, heavy physical exertion

### Mana Changes

Mana applies only to characters with a magical tradition — human wizards, witches (mostly human women, with smaller numbers of Draklid, Cephalen, Raknid, and rare freed Quell), and Primes. Most player characters have no mana resource at all. Do not deduct mana from non-casters.

Mana is spent only when a character actually casts a spell or channels magical power. Not spent for: thinking about magic, knowing magical things, using enchanted items that have their own power source.

- Trivial magic (lighting a wick, sensing nearby magic, minor moving of objects, small illusions): 0 to -2
- Moderate magic (combat spells, healing wounds, useful illusions, summoning small creatures, magical barriers): -5 to -10
- Major magic (large-scale destruction, major healing, terrain reshaping, powerful summons, breaking curses): -15 to -25
- Legendary magic (workings on the order of the Ensoulment): hundreds to many hundreds of mana. This is the scale of the great working that broke the Wartide. Player characters do not reach this tier through ordinary play, and the AI should not allow them to attempt it casually

Judge by what the story describes, not by the skill name. A fire-working to light a hearth is trivial. A fire-working to break a war-band is major. The same skill at different scales costs different amounts.

Failed spells still cost mana — the power was drawn and spent, it just didn't achieve the intended effect.

### Filling (witches)

A witch's reservoir does not refill from rest. Mana is gained by ingesting male essence or being ejaculated into, directly from a partner or in rare specialist preparations. In Avenor this is openly contractual through the Queen's College consort-line system. Outside Avenor it is hunted, runs through black markets, or is extracted from magical creatures by mana-hunters.

### Willpower Changes

Apply Willpower damage per the bands in resourceSettings.willpower whenever a Carnal Arts ability lands or non-Carnal pressure (fear, temptation, mind-craft, command-magic) hits an unresolved character. Crude-tier moves (-2 to -6), trained-tier (-8 to -20), master-tier finishers like Bind in Bliss, Break the Will, and Juicing (-25 to -60). Non-Carnal sources: intimidation rolls (-3 to -10), temptation offers (-3 to -10 per offer), mind-craft and command-magic (-15 to -40).

Apply standing debuffs by reducing the character's maximum Willpower for as long as the condition holds:

- Active addiction (drink, joyleaf, joy-wood, certain witch-preparations) lowers max while the craving is unsated; deeper addiction, larger cap reduction
- Recent humiliation, public shame, or recent torture imposes a temporary cap reduction until the character recovers a measure of self
- Presence in Hollow ground, Quell-touched ruins, deep Wartide-fragment sites drains Willpower over time as ambient pressure on anyone present

Apply status conditions as defense reductions — incoming Willpower damage at the high end of each band, or one band higher, depending on severity:

- Wet, Aphrodised, intoxicated: defense reduced against sexual pressure (and against temptation in the case of intoxication)
- Exhausted, bleeding, shamed: defense reduced against everything

Apply the passive ability Hardened to It as a damage reduction against sexual sources — knock incoming sexual Willpower damage down by one band (master to trained, trained to crude, crude to negligible).

Willpower does not regenerate in combat. It recovers fully on rest, provided the character can actually rest. A devout character at meditation or shrine, soothing salve, and similar narrative items aid recovery between scenes at narrator discretion.

## Don't Deduct Health When

- The story mentions treated or untreated wounds
- Story mentions pain from a wound
- When unconscious or carried
- An environment is generally dangerous, but does not explicitly damage the character
- A character is only looking around. Look around is a safe action.

## Don't Modify Resources When

- Player sleeps. Sleep is not a resource-modifying action.

narratorStyle: One good adjective beats three. Fragments are fine. A long sentence earns its length with momentum, not stacked clauses. One vivid detail beats three generic ones. The specific detail is always better than the label. Be funny, dry, impressed — but be something. Get in, make it land, get out. Build scenes from people, actions, and things that happen. Don't summarize states. No modern, scientific, or technological language. The prose should feel like a story told aloud. No word exists to impress. Every word exists to communicate.

death.instructions: The enemy or enemies assults the player sexually, using their body for their pleasure for some time. Describe this in detail. After absorbing enough semen, the player's body uses the magic of the semen to teleport itself to a safe place where they awaken. The character or party may have lost some or all of their possessions, which may have been looted or stolen or even taken by a trophy by a nemesis. This brush with death may leave a superficial physical mark on the characters (scars, mild deformities, etc., but not serious disabilities), and it should always have narrative consequences (for example, a loss of confidence in the player character or party's abilities), but the player character or party should be recovered to to full health and able to continue their adventure right away.

health.name: health
health.usageInstructions:
### Health Changes
- Small injuries: -1 to 15% player health
- Medium injuries: -5 to 25% player health
- Massive injuries: -15 to 50% of player health
- Normal Healing potions +30 - +40
- Bandages + 20
- Waking from unconsciousness: return to between half health and full health, depending on how well your recovery was.
- Being revived from death: return to full health.
### Don't Deduct Health When
- The story mentions treated or untreated wounds
- Story mentions pain from a wound
- When unconscious or carried
- An environment is generally dangerous, but does not explicitly damage the character
- A character is only looking around. Look around is a safe action.

stamina.name: stamina
stamina.usageInstructions:
### Stamina

The physical resource for nearly every character in Hedoria. Drained by combat, athletics, hard travel, and any sustained physical effort. Recharges per turn and replenishes on rest.

- Walking, talking, casual movement: 0
- Single combat strike, leap, sprint: -3 to -5
- Sustained engagement, long climb, hard ride: -5 to -10
- Pushing past collapse: -10+

Fighters, mercenaries, hunters, scouts, and the great mass of non-casters spend stamina on the things mana-users would spend mana on. A wizard or witch also spends stamina for physical actions; mana is for the working itself.

mana.name: mana
mana.usageInstructions:
### Mana (the wizard's reservoir)

The small magical reservoir produced by a wizard's body. Generated only by male humans with the spark — Brothers of Telinor, hedge-wizards, and the Avenorian consort line. Other characters do not generate mana and should not have this resource.

Mana recovers from rest overnight. The pool is small.

### Costs

- Trivial wizardry (a small ward, a brief stiffening): 0 to -2
- Standard wizardry through the staff (a strike behind plate, a brace against a charge): -5 to -10
- Major wizardry through the staff (Hardened Strike, Wind-Slow across a battlefield, Standing Wall against a sustained press): -15 to -25
- Without a staff in hand: utility costs roughly the same; combat-staff abilities are unavailable until the wizard replaces it.

reservoir.name: reservoir
reservoir.usageInstructions:
### Reservoir (the witch's stored mana)

The witch's stored magical pool. Does not regenerate from rest; a depleted witch must fill before working again. The cap is 10,000 — far beyond what any single witch reaches in ordinary play, but the ceiling is there because it has been touched.

Available to any character trained in witchcraft. Most others cannot draw on a witch-sized reservoir at all.

### Filling

The reservoir is filled either by drinking alchemical preparations distilled from real essence-sources, or directly from a willing male partner through ingestion of his essence or being ejaculated into. Specific filling paths, cultural framings, and their consequences live in the witchcraft skill and the relevant abilities — not here. Material alone (feathers, antlers, plant reagents) does not fill; only essence fills.

### Costs

- Trivial workings (witchlight, hedge-craft, small heat or cold, easing a fever): 0 to -5
- Moderate workings (combat projection, scry, telekinesis, conjured flame, glamour over self): -10 to -25
- Major workings (battlefield-scale fire or cold, glamour over a crowd, breaking a curse, mending a critical wound): -40 to -100
- Reservoir Spend (the boss-fight finisher): -100 to -300, often emptying the tank for a single act the world remembers

### Spent

A witch with low reservoir can do trivial workings only. All other workings: Very Hard minimum. A witch with an empty reservoir is a woman in unusual clothes hoping nobody notices. Witches in places where the practice is illegal live with this state as a constant condition.

willpower.name: willpower
willpower.usageInstructions:
### Willpower

The reserve a character holds against pressure that targets the mind and the body's reflexes rather than the body itself. Stands between intent and the act when something or someone tries to move you against your judgment — fear, charm, temptation, command, trained social or sexual pressure. Drained by all such attempts. Does not regenerate in combat. Recovers fully on rest, provided the character can actually rest.

### Damage sources

- Fear and threat (intimidation, oppressive places, a serious enemy's presence): -3 to -10
- Crude social or sexual pressure (taunts, low-tier seduction, public groping): -2 to -6
- Temptation (an offer that hits a craving): -3 to -10 per offer resisted; failure to resist drains the same amount and the character takes the bait
- Trained social or sexual pressure (polished interrogation, expert seduction, persuasion against your interests): -8 to -20
- Mind-craft and command-magic (compulsions, binding-work, master mind-workers): -15 to -40
- Will-shattering finishers (rare master-tier moves that exist to break someone): -25 to -60, more against an already-weakened target

### Direction of damage

Willpower damage falls on the **target** of pressure, not the source. A character who uses Carnal Arts, intimidation, charm, persuasion, or other Willpower-targeting moves shakes their target's Willpower — their own is not touched by the act of using the ability. The defender takes the damage; the attacker does not.

The narrator must identify who is acting and who is being acted upon each time these moves come into play. A courtesan working a room, brushing a cheek, or kissing a patron is using Carnal Arts on the patron — the patron's Willpower drains, not the courtesan's. The attacker only loses Willpower if the target counters back, or if a separate source (fear, temptation, etc.) acts on them.

### Standing debuffs

- Active addiction lowers maximum Willpower while the craving is unsated; deeper addictions impose larger caps
- Recent humiliation, public shame, or torture may impose a temporary cap reduction at narrator discretion until the character recovers a measure of self
- Some places drain Willpower over time as ambient pressure — see the location's own description

### Empty

A character at zero Willpower yields to whatever pressure brought them there. The narrator chooses the resolution that fits the source and the breaker's intent. This is not death and rarely permanent; the broken character recovers Willpower with rest, but the choices made at zero are remembered and acted upon.

### Recovery and modifiers

- Rest restores fully where rest is possible
- Specific abilities, items, and shrines may aid recovery — see their descriptions for effect
- An exhausted, bleeding, intoxicated, or shamed character defends worse against everything

### Coverage

Most flesh-bearing humanoids and most beasts carry Willpower. Mindless undead and constructs are immune. Species exceptions and creature inversions live in the species traits and bestiary entries; the resource itself is universal.
